sentences.text: At what point-- can  I talk?
No, not yet.
I'll let you know.
I want to go off the record.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Talking about Kaufering.
And there were one or two more places in and around Kaufering, where I was interned and worked.
But the next significant camp was Allach, A-L-L-A-C-H, which is also a-- an annex camp of Dachau.
No, not there.
They had just moved us.
They had moved us.
As a matter of fact, Birkenau was the only place where I had a chance to volunteer.
There was no other place in the three years that I was interned that I ever had chance to volunteer for a movement of-- from one camp to another.
So that was a singular event.
But we arrived in this camp, Allach, which was maybe 300 inmates.
And it was evident to me, right as I arrived, that people were sick.
And there was a large group of-- of inactivity in that camp.
This was in, I would say, late January of 1945.
History, of course, has it that by then, the Russians had come in to the eastern part of the front, including Auschwitz.
And many other things were happening on the southern part of the front, which we all did not know.
But it was apparent in the degree of the German guards.
And they even had guards that were what was called [NON-ENGLISH], which were retired, like reserve, army people.
Some of them were 60, 70 years old.
The others were-- were no longer guards.
They were either on some other front or had not survived.
So in this camp, that-- that was apparent.
There, most of the guards were older.
And right from the beginning of my arrival there, again because I spoke German, a guard picked me out and said, you are going to be in charge.
Every day, you're going to be bearing corpses that are going to be located, you know, in this and this place.
And we will have-- and they call that a [NON-ENGLISH] kommando.
And we will have a guard walk with you every day and show you where you are to dig.
Well, we did this the first day and dug a grave.
By the way, this was near a highway to Augsburg.

If you draw a line from Munich to Augsburg, this place where we went to more or less every day, within, you know, a few hundred feet, I'm not sure-- I could probably pick it out, if unless they've already found it.
Because the graves were probably not very deep.
They were fairly shallow.
But we dug graves.
There were six, seven of us with picks.
And the stones in Bavaria are-- or rather, the soil in Bavaria is composed of stones, rather than just sandy soil.
So digging a grave there was trickier, having to remove large stones, and by hand even, you know, some of them the size of a head.
And-- but we managed to dig about an eight by eight feet by six or so feet deep daily.
And it so happened that daily, in turn, there were enough corpses to fit into this large hole, which we then covered.
And then the next day, we would come with a new batch.
This was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] particularly, how shall I say, what was unusual about the procedure.
Procedure is a bad word.

What was unusual about this task was that more often than not, one of the corpses in the-- in the buggy that'd be carried, in the cart that'd be carried in, was one of the guys that helped to dig the grave the day before.

And it occurred to me that, you know, if I didn't survive this-- this situation-- it was early spring but it was winter, and things were cold-- things were not pleasant-- that I might very well end up being in one of those graves myself.
But it did not happen.
I did survive.
And towards the end of April, we-- we did not continue this process.
Because one morning, as an example, one morning the-- there were no guards at all around the camp.
And the Germans had left.
And I remember hearing shooting, and cannons, and bombs.
And so we knew that something was happening.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were maybe 50 people alive in the camp, which originally was 300 people.

And we-- that's when we took potatoes-- we knew where they were stored-- and would make fires by using even old barracks, old bedposts, or old posts and wood, lumber, that we could find, and roast the potatoes in the ashes of and embers of those fires.
That was our only food.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That lasted about four or five days.
[SIGHS] Then one day, May 1, early in the morning, the guards having left, there was an American jeep that came into the center of the courtyard of the camp.
And-- officer, with a driver, and the officer put his foot on the pavement.
And I walked towards him.
And he said, who are you?
And I identified myself.
And he said, where are the German guards?
Where are the Germans, SS?
And I said they had been gone for four days.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Was he speaking to you in English?
He was speaking to me in English.
And I understood English.
And I-- as I walked up to him, the first-- the first of my senses that reacted was his aftershave lotion.
I had not really smelled any aftershave lotion in a long time.
And It was kind of unusual that that would be a symbolic reaction.
Anyway, this officer, he was a young man, said, where are the rest of the inmates?
And I showed him.
He walked with me.
And he saw they were, you know, dozens of-- of corpses, cadavers, and maybe 20 or 30 or 40 people that he saw that were barely moving around.
There were maybe only eight people that were able to move like myself.
And he said to his driver to call his headquarters to set up a set of guards around the camp to quarantine it.
Why?

Because it was pretty obvious to him with all the cadavers and-- and other people sick that a typhus or any other kind of sickness would be rampant, or would become rampant, which was then set up that same day.
And before the American guards were stationed in place, I walked out of the camp.
That same day.
It all happen on May 1.
Well, I remember that I figured, why should I wait for formalities or any of that?
And I was feeling pretty good.
I had had some potatoes in me, and I'd had some water.
And I was all by myself.
And I climbed-- the camp had kind of a-- a-- a series of-- of earth mounds around it.
And I was just climbing up one of those mounds and kept on walkin'.
And I walked to a-- walked to about a mile across fields-- the camp was in the middle of nowhere-- across some fields, and maybe two miles.
And I came to a house.
I knocked on the door.
And a woman answered, came to the front door, and realized I, you know, was from some camp.
And she said, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH], you know, where do you come from?
And I answered her in perfect German.
And I said, you know where I came from, because you-- you know that there's a camp there.
It's pretty close to you, and things are pretty bad.
People are dying there and have died.
And I want to take a bath.
And I want some clothes.
And I need some food.
So she--
Oh, yes.
And she let me in-- and all in German.
She didn't understand why I spoke German.
And I took a bath.
And they all sat down with me and had a meal.
And they said they had a son that was on the front somewhere in Russia or wherever-- maybe not Russia, maybe it was a prison in Russia-- and that the husband was a-- was a village doctor.
And they said, you can sleep over here, and you can stay with us.
You know, we'd had no idea this was going on.
Well, during that night-- and I had clothes on from that son-- during that night, the same lieutenant that had freed me, and the rest of his group, came and took command of the house.
That became their HQ.
And they saw me.
And he said, how come you're here?
You're not supposed to be here?
You're supposed to be in quarantine.
And you're supposed to be there.
And I said, well, I bypassed that.
And-- but I can be of help to you if you are going to be active here.
I can be your interpreter.
And anyway, they all liked me.
And I stayed with that American company, and-- Thunderbird Division, by the way, Thunderbird Division, Third Army.
They ended up being special service and PX.
And I interpreted for them for five months.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was my end of the war.
Of course, more happen afterwards.
But it's the way I ended it.
They stayed in and around Bavaria.
And they made side trips.
Matter of fact, McIntyre was one of the-- they arranged for rest, and recreation, and entertainment, and food.
They became a PX and special service company, this particular group that had come up from-- from Italy.
I was like a mascot to them.
And I interpreted both in French and German, because we went into France, too, with a series of convoys.
And we requisitioned some champagne in Reims, which was not far from there, and other places.
And I was always able to help them communicate.
I didn't at that moment.
I was being very selfish.
I didn't.
I didn't think of my father, mother, or sister.
I did eventually, but not at that moment.
I was just kind of feeling my freedom.
It's like a butterfly, you-- you know, you've been catching all the time.
And all of a sudden, you-- you open it.
And I-- I was-- I was feeling my wings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I must say it was probably selfish, but I'm not sorry.
Because subsequently, later that year, I did get in touch with all those that survived in my family.
Staying with this company, from May until about September, beginning of September, we traveled throughout Europe.
And by then, they were shipped home.
They were being shipped home.
And they all left from the Normandy coast of France, which was on the eastern part of Normandy, which was set up and organized to make arrangements for troops to come home.
And I went with them until the very end.
And they said, well, we-- we can take you.
But it will-- it will involve some legal, you know, problems.
And you may be better off in coming in a different way.
But we ended up separating.
And then I hitched a ride from-- from that particular part of Normandy back into Paris.
And having been in Paris before the war, I knew Paris, and connected with that cousin of mine who I-- I knew where she lived, and happened to run into her on the street.
And it was incredible.
I was in an American uniform without badges, but still an American uniform.
She wouldn't-- she didn't recognize me at first.
And--
I recognized her.
And it-- you know, she said to me, it's-- it's amazing that here you are.
It's like a-- a dead coming alive, because we had all taken you for dead.
You know, the war was over in May and this is September, and-- and what happened?
So I explained it.
And she said that, well, you know, your sister is-- is worried to death.
She lives in England, is married now.
And your mother survived.
And she is here in Eastern Germany someplace, where my brother-in-law was an officer in the Royal Air Force.
I was able to-- to put her.
And I then eventually touched base with all of them.
Matter of fact, my mother took a train to London to the-- you know, there's a train that goes to London by way of a ferry from France to London, which is now the tunnel.
And I saw her in that train before she went on that ferry, in-- when was it-- in very end of 1945.
And that's the first time I'd seen my mother since 1939.
So here, I was a child in '39, not even 14.
And now in '45, I was 19.
So the difference of five years or so made me-- I was a man, and I left as a child.
And it was-- she might not have recognized me, but she did.
And it was a rather unusual meeting.
It was just the two of us.
I knew what train she would be on.
And this was later in 1945.
Subsequently, I went to visit her and my sister in-- in London.
Well, there was no one to-- to help the-- it was just she and I, instead of maybe having someone say, here, Jerry, you know, lead me on, and-- and have my mother have somebody there.
It was unusual in that sense, and that we had to find each other.
And my mother was full of emotion, as I was.
By then, I learned also, as I had before, that my father was, you know, not among the living.
My father had been caught during a-- an arrest period in southern France in, sometime in 1943, and had the bad luck of having on him both his real and false papers.

Because by then, in-- in the late '42s and on until the end of the war, people would assume wrong names, or assumed names with fake papers-- which my father did as well, tried to evade arrest and avoid arrest.
But he had both his passports on him.
So he was arrested and sent to Auschwitz.
And apparently, he didn't come back.
When I say apparently, I know he didn't come back.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was also very distraught when he heard, in '42, that I was arrested out of the home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming back to the beginning of my story.
Let's see.
Who told me about him-- someone who was with him, the son of someone who was with him who now lives in Chicago.
And it is someone that he knew before the war-- I had not known him-- that survived.
Yes.
Yeah, my aunt--
Ilse Weiner.
My mother's sister had always been fond of me since I was a small child.
And when she heard that I had survived, that I was in Paris-- by then I had taken a-- I-- I was living in an apartment, and I had gone to school, and I had a job, and all that.
She sent me papers, an affidavit, to come to San Francisco, even though at first I didn't wish to.
She had emigrated before the war.
She had emigrated in '38, before the war.
That's a different aunt.
No, that's a different aunt.
Oh, that one-- that aunt in Paris, She-- I kept in touch with her.
She was a widow.
Her husband had died.
She-- she was never in Germany.
She was--
No, I know, but in France.
In France?
She was hidden.
Ok.
She was able to escape the-- I don't know how-- the pursuit of the Germans.
She was never caught.
Her name was Lee Reinis, R-E-I-N-I-S, originally [NON-ENGLISH].
Oh, I changed my name from-- when I came from France to this country, the day after I got here, by going to the social security service and give them a different name.
Because my name was Gerhardt Rosenzweig, it could not be pronounced in French.
And I had problem spelling the name to them and they spelling it.
And I made the decision at the time to change the name, which I'm-- I've been sorry for for the last 30 some years.
I have to excuse myself, but I have to go to the bathroom.
OK, I came to United States in February of '47.
And that is to join an aunt in San Francisco, and husband, whom I had met as a child in Germany.
I had not seen her since 1937 or '38.
And I lived in her house, although I got a job and eventually moved out.
Moved-- they lived in Sausalito, which is a community north of San Francisco, first community north of San Francisco.
And it was quite a marvel for me to see that community as I arrived from-- from Europe, because it is so idyllic.
And my first impression has never left me of that particular place.
And I set up a-- I went to school.
And I took a job.
And I-- what else did I do?
I got married too soon.
I met my wife and had children.
What was- what was her name?
Her name was Paulette.

She was a Belgian-born Jewish girl who had managed to leave Belgium in, I think, beginning of 1940 during the war, with her mother and her sister-- went to Portugal and then came to the United States.
But I met her during one social.
I belonged to various social groups in San Francisco at the time.
And what we had in common was we both spoke French.
I lived in San Francisco of more or less February of '47 until May of '48, over a year, year and two months.
And then through a fluke, I was able to move to Los Angeles, where someone proposed a position for me that they would-- they knew I could fulfill.
And they would give me a draw so that I would have expenses covered.
And it amounted to selling display materials to stores.
I had no experience in that.
And I tried it.
And somehow, I'm still in that business, in a-- in a changed way.
But I'm still dealing with one client at this point and am designing and helping to manufacture countertop display fixtures that are being used, you know, on a permanent basis.
In a store, right, in a specialty store-- Nordstrom.
I'm still doing that.
Other things?
I'm-- my biggest hobby is boating.
I'm a boater.
And I-- we have-- this is our sixth boat now.
And matter of fact, we just came back from Mexico.
We do long range boating, as well as close by boating, 55-foot boat.
My own 55-foot boat that I sail, powerboat.
I sail it, but it's-- it's not fabric sail.
It's still called sailing, but it's not that kind of sailing.
And we have children.
We have two children from my first marriage to the lady I married in San Francisco.
That marriage lasted about 10 years.
And there were two children.
Bob-- Robert and Steve.
Robert was born in 1949.
He spent four years in the Army, in-- or the Navy in Vietnam, between college period and is living in Los Angeles and is not married.
And then Steve, his brother, was born in '51.
He's 44 years old.
And he's married.
He became a physician.
He is a nuclear, doctor of nuclear medicine, works at the VA hospital in San Francisco.
That's where they live.
They have two children, two of our grandchildren.
And their names are Julie and Benjamin.
Julie is going to be six.
And Benjamin is three.
And Steve's wife's name is Trudy.
And my current wife is Betty.
Current-- I-- we've been married since '72, so it's 20-- 23 years, and happily married.
And Betty has two children from her previous marriage.
And they are Michael, who was born in '50-- '54, I believe, and his brother, David, who is a successful realtor and businessman, and has two children, also.
And David's children-- his wife's name is Suzanne.
And his children are Garrett and Britain-- girl and a boy.
So each of our kids that have children have one of each.
Well, the one thing I feel I must add, it might be controversial.
But I'd like to add it.
And that is, in-- in this interview, I don't know how much time you have.
I am-- I am thankful, grateful, respectful of being able to get the chance to tell part of my story.

What has happened in-- in recent years, and maybe even not so recent years, is that people who were fortunate to survive the war and are survivors by either being hidden or by having left the-- the countries involved in the war, and come to either Shanghai, or come to Australia, or come to the United States or Israel, cannot be categorized the same way as those such as myself, who have survived the war by witnessing what I have seen.
And that is the one difference I would like to make.
I saw it.
And I can't even describe it that well.
It is totally indescribable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that is the Holocaust, the word that Elie Weisel coined, because it-- it had to do with fire.
And it has to do with the annihilation and genocide.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Sorry, but I had to put that in.
